one_liner: A 32-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of intense thirst and urinating 'every hour, day and night.' Symptoms began shortly after a motor vehicle accident in which he sustained a head injury.

stage2.hpi_extension: He describes producing **huge volumes of dilute, clear urine** — estimated 7-8 liters per day — and drinking ice water continuously to keep up. He wakes 4-5 times per night to urinate. He craves cold water in particular. He denies polyphagia, weight loss, or weight gain. Three weeks ago he was in a motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness; head CT showed a small **basilar skull fracture** but no intracranial hemorrhage. He was discharged after observation.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student. Non-smoker, rare alcohol, no drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: General: alert, appears thirsty and is holding a water bottle. HEENT: **dry mucous membranes**. Cardiovascular: mild tachycardia, no murmur. Abdomen: soft, non-tender. Neuro: cranial nerves intact, strength and sensation normal, no focal deficits. Visual fields grossly intact. Skin: decreased skin turgor.

lab_results.result: **Sodium 149 mEq/L (elevated)**, potassium 4.1, chloride 110, bicarbonate 24, **BUN 22, creatinine 0.9**. Glucose 92. **Serum osmolality 302 mOsm/kg (elevated)**.
lab_results.clinical_significance: **Hypernatremia with elevated serum osmolality** in the setting of massive urine output reflects free water loss. Normal glucose excludes hyperglycemic diuresis.

lab_results.result: **Urine specific gravity 1.002**. **Urine osmolality 95 mOsm/kg (inappropriately low)**. Glucose negative, ketones negative, protein negative. 24-hour urine volume 7.2 L.
lab_results.clinical_significance: An **inappropriately dilute urine** (osmolality <300 mOsm/kg) in the face of serum hyperosmolality is pathognomonic for **diabetes insipidus**. The distinction between central and nephrogenic DI requires a desmopressin trial.

lab_results.result: **Water deprivation test**: after 4 hours, serum osmolality rises to 308 while **urine osmolality remains 105 mOsm/kg**. After administration of **desmopressin (DDAVP)**, **urine osmolality rises >50% to 420 mOsm/kg**.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Failure to concentrate urine with water deprivation confirms DI. A **robust (>50%) rise in urine osmolality after DDAVP** localizes the defect to **central DI** (ADH deficiency). Nephrogenic DI would show little or no response to DDAVP.

stage5.final_clue: MRI of the pituitary shows **loss of the normal T1 'bright spot'** of the posterior pituitary, consistent with post-traumatic injury to the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. He is started on **intranasal desmopressin** with prompt resolution of polyuria and normalization of serum sodium.

debrief.classic_presentation: Central diabetes insipidus presents with **polyuria (often >3 L/day), polydipsia, and nocturia**. Patients characteristically **crave ice-cold water**. Serum sodium is normal or mildly elevated because most patients can match their fluid losses if given free access to water — but **restricted water access rapidly produces hypernatremia** and volume depletion. Precipitating causes include **head trauma, pituitary/hypothalamic surgery, craniopharyngioma or other suprasellar masses, infiltrative disease (sarcoid, Langerhans cell histiocytosis), and idiopathic autoimmune disease**.
debrief.pathophysiology: DI results from an inability to concentrate urine. **Central DI** is caused by **ADH (arginine vasopressin) deficiency** from damage to the hypothalamic supraoptic/paraventricular nuclei or posterior pituitary. **Nephrogenic DI** is caused by renal resistance to ADH (drugs such as lithium/demeclocycline, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, hereditary V2 receptor or aquaporin-2 mutations). Without ADH action at the V2 receptor of the collecting duct, aquaporin-2 channels are not inserted, free water reabsorption fails, and dilute urine is produced.
debrief.treatment_overview: **Central DI**: **intranasal or oral desmopressin (DDAVP)**, a synthetic V2-selective ADH analog, restores urinary concentration. Adequate free water intake is essential. **Nephrogenic DI**: remove offending drugs, correct electrolytes, use **thiazide diuretics (paradoxical antidiuretic effect)**, a low-salt/low-protein diet, and sometimes amiloride (for lithium-induced nephrogenic DI).
high_yield_pearls: **Water deprivation test** is the gold standard: in DI, urine fails to concentrate despite rising serum osmolality. DDAVP then distinguishes central (responds) from nephrogenic (no response).
Central DI causes: **trauma, pituitary surgery, tumors (craniopharyngioma), histiocytosis X, granulomatous disease, autoimmune/idiopathic**.
Nephrogenic DI causes: **lithium, demeclocycline, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, hereditary V2 receptor mutations**.
On pituitary MRI, central DI classically shows **loss of the posterior pituitary 'bright spot'** on T1-weighted imaging.
DI vs SIADH: in DI the problem is too little ADH effect (dilute urine, rising sodium); in SIADH it is too much ADH (concentrated urine, falling sodium).
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Endocrine chapter, 'Diabetes insipidus' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Renal chapter, 'ADH — Vasopressin/V2 receptor' section.
